subsystem: nanopb (Protocol Buffers for embedded systems) wire-format decoder. This is the static-storage field decoder decode_static_field() from pb_decode.c, dispatched per-field while parsing an incoming protobuf message into a fixed C struct. It handles the four nanopb 'how-type' storage classes (REQUIRED, OPTIONAL, REPEATED, ONEOF) and drives the actual scalar/submessage decoder function chosen from the PB_DECODERS table.
provenance: Identified by nanopb-specific API surface: pb_istream_t, pb_wire_type_t, pb_field_iter_t, pb_type_t, pb_decoder_t, the PB_DECODERS[] dispatch table, the PB_HTYPE()/PB_LTYPE() macro pair, PB_HTYPE_REQUIRED/OPTIONAL/REPEATED/ONEOF, PB_WT_STRING, PB_LTYPE_LAST_PACKABLE, PB_LTYPE_SUBMESSAGE, pb_make_string_substream/pb_close_string_substream, pb_message_set_to_defaults, and the PB_RETURN_ERROR macro. The 'checkreturn' attribute, pb_size_t, and iter->pos/pData/pSize field-iterator layout are unique to Kapsi/nanopb. The header comment 'sample: primevul_c_train_2942_vuln' marks it as a PrimeVul training sample; that label is untrusted metadata and does not change the blind analysis.
trust_boundary: The pb_istream_t 'stream' carries fully attacker-controlled bytes: a protobuf message from network/IPC/file. The 'wire_type' argument is parsed directly from the attacker's field key (varint tag), so it is hostile and can be any of the wire-type values. In contrast, 'iter->pos' points at the COMPILE-TIME-GENERATED pb_field_t descriptor for the message (type, data_size, array_size, tag, ptr) and iter->pData/iter->pSize point into the caller-allocated destination struct; those are trusted-by-construction. The security question is therefore: given a trusted field descriptor but a hostile stream and hostile wire_type, can the attacker drive an out-of-bounds write, a bad indirect call, or a lifetime bug in the destination struct?

what_the_code_does.explanation: decode_static_field is the per-field decoder for statically-allocated (non-pointer, non-callback) fields. It takes the input stream, the wire_type read from the attacker's field key, and a field iterator whose iter->pos is the generated descriptor for the current field. Line 7 loads the packed 'type' word from that descriptor. Line 8 extracts the low-level type via PB_LTYPE(type) and uses it as an index into the PB_DECODERS[] function-pointer table to select the concrete scalar/string/submessage decoder (func). This func is the routine that will actually consume bytes from the stream and write them into the destination. All subsequent branches ultimately call func; the only thing that varies is HOW MANY times and into WHICH address.

The switch dispatches on PB_HTYPE(type), the 'how-type' storage class from the descriptor. For a REQUIRED scalar field the code simply invokes the selected decoder once, writing directly into iter->pData (the field's slot inside the destination struct). There is no has-flag and no counter for required fields, so the descriptor's data_size and the decoder's own bounds are the only constraints.

For an OPTIONAL field, nanopb keeps a separate 'has_x' boolean whose address is iter->pSize. Line 16 guards the degenerate case where pSize and pData coincide (some layouts have no separate has-flag); when they differ, line 17 writes the has-flag to true, then line 18 decodes the value into iter->pData. The pSize/pData pointers come from the trusted descriptor/struct layout, so the write on line 17 targets a known bool slot.

REPEATED fields split into two sub-cases. When the attacker sent this field with wire type PB_WT_STRING (length-delimited) AND the field's low-type is packable (a fixed scalar), nanopb treats it as a PACKED array: many scalar values concatenated inside one length-delimited blob. Line 26 aliases iter->pSize as a pb_size_t* count pointer (the current element count for this repeated field). Lines 28-30 open a bounded substream that spans exactly the length-delimited payload; pb_make_string_substream reads the length prefix and sets substream.bytes_left, so the packed loop cannot read past the declared blob length.

The packed decode loop runs while there are bytes left in the substream AND the current element count *size is strictly less than the descriptor's array_size (the compiled capacity of the destination array). Line 34 computes the write target pItem = base + data_size * (*size), i.e. the address of element number *size. Line 35 decodes one element into pItem; on failure it breaks. Line 40 increments the count only after a successful decode. Because the loop condition checks *size < array_size BEFORE computing pItem and writing, indices are confined to 0 .. array_size-1, so the per-element write stays inside the destination array.

After the loop, line 43-44 enforce that the packed blob was fully consumed: if bytes remain it means the attacker supplied MORE packed elements than array_size (the loop exited on the *size cap while data was left), which is reported as 'array overflow'. This trailing check is the second half of the bound: the loop guard prevents writing past capacity, and this check turns an over-long array into an error rather than silently dropping data. Line 45-46 close the substream (restoring the parent stream's byte accounting), and line 48 returns the accumulated status.

The other REPEATED sub-case handles a NON-packed repeated field: each occurrence of the field on the wire appends exactly one element. Line 53 aliases the count, line 54 computes pItem at index *size, and line 56 post-increments *size while checking the pre-increment value against array_size: if the old count was already >= array_size the function errors out with 'array overflow' before decoding. When the check passes (old count in 0 .. array_size-1) line 59 decodes one element into pItem. Note that pItem on line 54 is formed from *size BEFORE the bound is checked, but it is only DEREFERENCED (inside func) on line 59, which is reached only after the check passes.

For a ONEOF field (a union where at most one member is set), line 63 records which member is now active by writing the field's tag into the which_x discriminator at iter->pSize. If the active member is a submessage, lines 68-69 zero the entire union storage (data_size bytes) so any stale pointers/callbacks become NULL, then apply the submessage's default values via pb_message_set_to_defaults. Line 71 then decodes the member into iter->pData. The memset+defaults establish a clean initial state for the newly selected union member before decoding.

Any PB_HTYPE(type) that is not one of the four handled storage classes is rejected as 'invalid field type'. Because type comes from the trusted generated descriptor rather than the wire, this default is a defensive guard against a corrupt/unsupported descriptor rather than an attacker-reachable path.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The concrete decoder is chosen by indexing the PB_DECODERS[] function-pointer table with PB_LTYPE(type), and the result is later called indirectly (lines 13/18/35/59/71). This is only safe if PB_LTYPE(type) is guaranteed to be a valid index into PB_DECODERS and 'type' truly comes from a trusted, well-formed generated descriptor. If the field descriptor table can ever be attacker-influenced, mismatched against the message definition, or contain an LTYPE value outside the decoder table's bounds (e.g. via a version skew between generated headers and the runtime, or memory corruption), line 8 is an out-of-bounds table read yielding a bogus function pointer, and the subsequent func(...) call transfers control to an attacker-influenced or garbage address. The invariant the implementation must uphold: PB_LTYPE(type) < (number of entries in PB_DECODERS) for every descriptor, and descriptors are read-only/trusted. This is a latent contract in code not shown (PB_DECODERS definition); nanopb enforces it at codegen time, so exploitability depends on descriptor integrity.

The packed-array write on line 34-35 stays in bounds ONLY because of the loop guard *size < iter->pos->array_size on line 32. The attacker fully controls how many packed elements are present (by choosing the length-delimited blob length) and the byte content of each. The safety contract is: (a) array_size in the descriptor must exactly match the real capacity of the array at iter->pData, and (b) data_size must be the true stride of that array. If a generated descriptor ever understates the element count needed or overstates array_size relative to the actually-allocated struct field, this loop performs an out-of-bounds write of attacker-controlled bytes into the destination struct (CWE-787). The check as written is correct; the residual risk is descriptor/struct-layout correctness, which nanopb guarantees by codegen. Also note *size is trusted to start at 0 for a fresh message; a caller that reuses a struct without resetting the count could begin writing past prior elements.

In the non-packed repeated case, pItem is COMPUTED from *size on line 54 before the bound is validated on line 56. Forming a pointer past the end of the array (when *size >= array_size) is technically undefined behavior even though the pointer is not dereferenced until after the check passes, and the multiplication iter->pos->data_size * (*size) is pb_size_t arithmetic that could wrap if data_size and *size were both large. In practice *size is bounded by array_size (a trusted, typically small descriptor constant), and the error path on line 57 returns before func() dereferences pItem, so no out-of-bounds WRITE occurs; the observable effect is limited to computing (not using) an out-of-range pointer. The invariant: pItem must never be dereferenced when *size >= array_size, which the current control flow upholds. Best practice is to compute pItem only after the bound check; the ordering here is fragile against future edits.

The ONEOF handler unconditionally zeroes the union storage on line 68 when a submessage member is selected. If the SAME oneof was already populated earlier in the same message (an attacker may send the oneof field twice, or send two different members), the previously-decoded member is overwritten with zeros WITHOUT first releasing any resources it owned. For a purely static submessage this is harmless, but if the earlier member (or a nested field within it) held a pointer or a resource that requires pb_release, the memset drops the reference and leaks it (CWE-401), and more generally overwriting a live discriminator/value pair on repeated oneof occurrences is a classic nanopb hazard. The invariant the implementation must uphold: before switching or re-decoding a oneof member, any previously-owned dynamic content must be released, and re-sending a oneof must be handled coherently. This is a latent contract; whether it bites depends on whether oneof members can own releasable resources in the built configuration.

For REQUIRED and OPTIONAL fields the function calls func() with the attacker-controlled stream but performs no check here that wire_type is compatible with the field's expected wire encoding (that gating is expected to happen in the caller, decode_field/pb_decode). If a caller ever reached decode_static_field with a mismatched wire_type for a scalar field, the selected decoder would interpret the stream under the wrong framing (e.g. treating a length-delimited blob as a varint or vice versa), which for length-prefixed types is a path to over-reads or mis-sized copies inside func. The invariant: the caller must validate wire_type against PB_LTYPE(type) before dispatching (only the packed branch on lines 21-22 does any wire_type check locally). This is defensive-context, not a bug in the shown code, but it is the contract this function depends on.

summary: This is nanopb's decode_static_field, the per-field protobuf decoder into fixed C structs. The array-bound logic is actually correct: the packed loop is guarded by both the *size < array_size cap and a trailing 'bytes left = overflow' check, and the non-packed case bound-checks before dereferencing. The residual risks are latent contracts in code not shown: the PB_DECODERS[PB_LTYPE(type)] indirect-dispatch depends on descriptor integrity, the packed/non-packed writes depend on descriptor array_size/data_size matching the real struct, the oneof memset can drop a previously-set member's resources on a duplicated/switched oneof, and the function trusts the caller to have validated wire_type for scalar fields. No clearly attacker-reachable out-of-bounds write is present given trusted descriptors; this is a low-to-moderate-risk sample whose safety rests on invariants upheld elsewhere.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Reads the packed type word from the trusted generated descriptor; not attacker-controlled.
Function-pointer table indexed by PB_LTYPE(type) then called indirectly; OOB index / bad-descriptor risk.
Dispatch on trusted descriptor how-type; default case rejects unknown values.
Decodes with attacker stream and no local wire_type compatibility check; relies on caller gating.
Writes a single bool at a trusted struct offset guarded by pSize != pData; bounded.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: packed guard wire_type == PB_WT_STRING && PB_LTYPE(type) <= PB_LTYPE_LAST_PACKABLE (lines 21-22)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Correctly restricts packed handling to length-delimited framing of packable scalar types.
Aliases the count slot from trusted layout; the risk is in how the count bounds writes, covered below.
Establishes a length-bounded substream so packed decode cannot exceed the declared blob length; correct.
This guard is the sole in-bounds guarantee for the packed write; depends on descriptor array_size/data_size correctness.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: packed pItem = pData + data_size * (*size) + func write (lines 34-35)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Attacker-controlled bytes written at computed index; bounded by line 32 guard, latent OOB if descriptor wrong.
Increment only after successful decode, inside the *size<array_size guard; cannot exceed capacity.
Correctly turns an over-long packed array into an error instead of silent truncation; a mitigation, not a defect.
Restores parent stream accounting; no attacker-controlled sizing here.
Pointer formed from *size before the bound check on line 56; UB/overflow-arithmetic concern though not dereferenced until after check.
Post-increment compare correctly rejects when old count >= capacity; write on line 59 only on the passing path.
Writes the which_x tag to a trusted union discriminator slot; bounded.
Zeroes a possibly already-set oneof member without releasing owned resources; resource-leak / stale-state hazard on duplicated or switched oneof.
Defensive rejection of unknown how-type from a trusted descriptor; not attacker-reachable.

findings.title: Indirect decoder dispatch via PB_DECODERS[PB_LTYPE(type)] depends on descriptor integrity
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds table read feeding an indirect call (untrusted function pointer)
findings.invariant: PB_LTYPE(type) must always be a valid index into PB_DECODERS[], and the pb_field_t descriptor at iter->pos must be trusted, read-only, and version-matched with the runtime.
findings.failure_mode: If PB_LTYPE(type) exceeds the PB_DECODERS bound (descriptor/runtime version skew, corrupted descriptor table, or a build where LTYPE range and table size disagree), line 8 reads a function pointer out of bounds and the later func(...) calls (lines 13/18/35/59/71) transfer control to a bogus address. Under trusted, codegen-consistent descriptors this cannot happen; it is a latent contract, not a wire-reachable bug.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm PB_DECODERS has an entry for every PB_LTYPE value the codegen can emit, that PB_LTYPE(type) is masked to the table range, and that descriptor tables are const/read-only.

findings.title: Packed-array write bounded only by loop guard; safety rests on descriptor array_size/data_size matching the real struct
findings.anchor: while (substream.bytes_left > 0 && *size < iter->pos->array_size) { void *pItem = (char*)iter->pData + iter->pos->data_size * (*size); if (!func(&substream, iter->pos, pItem)) ...
findings.invariant: iter->pos->array_size must equal the true element capacity of the array at iter->pData, iter->pos->data_size must equal its element stride, and *size must be 0 (or the correct prior count) at entry for a fresh/decoded message.
findings.failure_mode: The attacker controls the number and content of packed elements via the length-delimited blob. The write at pItem stays in bounds only because of the *size < array_size guard on line 32. If a generated descriptor overstates array_size relative to the actual struct field, or a caller reuses a destination struct without resetting *size, attacker-controlled bytes are written past the array end (heap/stack/BSS corruption). With correct nanopb codegen and a freshly zeroed struct this is safe; the trailing bytes_left check (lines 43-44) additionally rejects over-long arrays.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm generated descriptors set array_size/data_size to the real field capacity/stride and that decode entry points zero the count fields before decoding.

findings.title: ONEOF submessage memset overwrites a previously-set member without releasing it
findings.bug_class: Resource leak / stale-state on duplicated or switched oneof member
findings.invariant: Before overwriting a oneof's storage for a newly selected (or re-sent) member, any releasable resources owned by the previously-active member must be released, and re-decoding the same oneof must be coherent.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker can send the same oneof field twice, or two different members of it, in one message. Line 68 unconditionally zeroes the union storage; if a previously-decoded member (or a nested field) owned a pointer/resource requiring pb_release, that reference is dropped and leaked, and the discriminator/value pair is rewritten each time. For purely static submessages this is benign; the exposure exists only when oneof members can own releasable resources in the build configuration.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether oneof members in the target build can own dynamically-allocated/callback resources, and whether the caller releases the prior member before decode_static_field re-runs for the same oneof.

findings.title: Non-packed repeated pItem computed from *size before the bounds check
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds pointer formation / unchecked size arithmetic (not dereferenced)
findings.invariant: pItem must never be dereferenced when *size >= array_size, and data_size * (*size) must not overflow pb_size_t before the element write.
findings.failure_mode: On line 54 pItem is formed at index *size before the guard on line 56 validates it; when *size >= array_size this forms an out-of-bounds pointer (technically UB) and the multiply could theoretically wrap for pathological data_size/*size. Because the error return on line 57 precedes the only dereference (func on line 59), no out-of-bounds write occurs with the current control flow; the concern is fragility against future edits and code-hygiene, not an exploitable primitive here.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm no path dereferences pItem when the bound check fails, and consider computing pItem after the check to remove the out-of-bounds pointer formation.

findings.title: Scalar REQUIRED/OPTIONAL decode trusts caller to have validated wire_type
findings.anchor: case PB_HTYPE_REQUIRED: return func(stream, iter->pos, iter->pData); ... case PB_HTYPE_OPTIONAL: ... return func(stream, iter->pos, iter->pData);
findings.bug_class: Missing local input validation (wire-type vs field-type mismatch)
findings.invariant: The caller (decode_field/pb_decode) must validate that the attacker-supplied wire_type is compatible with PB_LTYPE(type) before dispatching to decode_static_field for REQUIRED/OPTIONAL fields.
findings.failure_mode: Only the packed branch (lines 21-22) inspects wire_type locally. For REQUIRED/OPTIONAL scalars, func is invoked on the attacker stream regardless of wire_type; if a caller ever dispatched with a mismatched wire_type, the selected decoder would parse the stream under the wrong framing (e.g. length-delimited bytes as a varint), which for length-prefixed types can cause over-reads or mis-sized copies inside func. In stock nanopb the wire_type is validated upstream, so this is a documented dependency rather than a defect in the shown code.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the dispatcher that calls decode_static_field rejects wire_type/field-type mismatches before this function runs.

audit_checklist: Verify PB_DECODERS has a valid entry for every PB_LTYPE value emitted by codegen and that PB_LTYPE(type) can never index past it (descriptor/runtime version consistency).
Confirm iter->pos->array_size and iter->pos->data_size always match the real capacity and stride of the destination array for every generated message.
Confirm decode entry points zero (or correctly initialize) the pb_size_t count at iter->pSize before decoding so packed/non-packed writes start at index 0.
Check that the caller validates the attacker-supplied wire_type against the field's PB_LTYPE before dispatching REQUIRED/OPTIONAL scalar decodes.

Review oneof handling: ensure a previously-set oneof member (especially any with pointer/callback/dynamic content) is released before the memset on line 68 overwrites it, and that re-sent oneofs are handled coherently.
Verify pItem is never dereferenced on the array-overflow error path (line 54 forms the pointer before the line 56 check), and consider reordering to compute pItem after the bound check.
Confirm pb_make_string_substream/pb_close_string_substream correctly bound and restore stream byte accounting so packed decode cannot escape the declared blob length.
